revision.text:
'''木花知流比売'''（このはなちるひめ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神]]。
==概要== 古事記にのみ登場。[[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]は[[木花之佐久夜毘売]]や[[石長比売]]、[[神大市比売]]、[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|手名椎・足名椎]]などたくさんの神をもうけているが、木花知流比売もその一柱。
佐久夜毘売や石長比売と同一神とする説もある。
==系譜== [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]の娘の一人で、[[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]が大山津見神の孫の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛名田比売命]]を娶って産んだ神[[八島士奴美神]]との間に、[[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]]を産んでいる。
==関連項目== * [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] * [[八島士奴美神]] * [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] * [[木花佐久夜毘売]]

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月12日 (土) 08:20 (UTC)}} '''木花知流比売'''（このはなちるひめ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神]]。
==概要== 古事記にのみ登場。[[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]は[[木花之佐久夜毘売]]や[[石長比売]]、[[神大市比売]]、[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|手名椎・足名椎]]などたくさんの神をもうけているが、木花知流比売もその一柱。
佐久夜毘売や石長比売と同一神とする説もある。
==系譜== [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]の娘の一人で、[[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]が大山津見神の孫の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛名田比売命]]を娶って産んだ神[[八島士奴美神]]との間に、[[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]]を産んでいる。
==関連項目== * [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] * [[八島士奴美神]] * [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] * [[木花之佐久夜毘売]]

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月12日 (土) 08:20 (UTC)}} '''木花知流比売'''（このはなちるひめ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神]]。
==概要== [[古事記]]にのみ登場。[[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]は[[木花之佐久夜毘売]]や[[石長比売]]、[[神大市比売]]、[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|手名椎・足名椎]]などたくさんの神をもうけているが、木花知流比売もその一柱。八島士奴美神の妃となった。
佐久夜毘売や石長比売と同一神とする説、また佐久夜毘売（さくや）と知流比売（ちる）から対の神とする説もある。
==系譜== [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]の娘の一人で、[[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]が大山津見神の孫の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛名田比売命]]を娶って産んだ神[[八島士奴美神]]との間に、[[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]]を産んでいる。
==関連項目== * [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] * [[八島士奴美神]] * [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] * [[木花之佐久夜毘売]]
{{デフォルトソート:このはなちるひめ}} [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]]

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月12日 (土) 08:20 (UTC)}} '''木花知流比売'''（このはなちるひめ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神]]。
==概要== [[古事記]]にのみ登場。[[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]は[[木花之佐久夜毘売]]や[[石長比売]]、[[神大市比売]]、[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|手名椎・足名椎]]などたくさんの神をもうけているが、木花知流比売もその一柱。八島士奴美神の妃となった。
佐久夜毘売や石長比売と同一神とする説、また佐久夜毘売（さくや）と知流比売（ちる）から対の神とする説もある。
新潟市の海辺の森には木花知流姫と名づけられた桜がある。
==系譜== [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]の娘の一人で、[[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]が大山津見神の孫の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛名田比売命]]を娶って産んだ神[[八島士奴美神]]との間に、[[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]]を産んでいる。
==関連項目== * [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] * [[八島士奴美神]] * [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] * [[木花之佐久夜毘売]]
{{デフォルトソート:このはなちるひめ}} [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]]

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月12日 (土) 08:20 (UTC)}} '''木花知流比売'''（このはなちるひめ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[女神]]。
==概要== [[古事記]]にのみ登場。[[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]は[[木花之佐久夜毘売]]や[[石長比売]]、[[神大市比売]]、[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|手名椎・足名椎]]などたくさんの神をもうけているが、木花知流比売もその一柱。八島士奴美神の妃となった。
==系譜== [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]の娘の一人で、[[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]が大山津見神の孫の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛名田比売命]]を娶って産んだ神[[八島士奴美神]]との間に、[[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]]を産んでいる。
==関連項目== * [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] * [[八島士奴美神]] * [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] * [[木花之佐久夜毘売]]
{{デフォルトソート:このはなちるひめ}} [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]]

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月12日 (土) 08:20 (UTC)}} '''木花知流比売'''（このはなちるひめ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[女神]]。
==概要== [[古事記]]にのみ登場。[[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]は[[木花之佐久夜毘売]]や[[石長比売]]、[[神大市比売]]、[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|手名椎・足名椎]]などたくさんの神をもうけているが、木花知流比売もその一柱。八島士奴美神の妃となった。
==系譜== [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]の娘の一人で、[[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]が大山津見神の孫の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛名田比売命]]を娶って産んだ神[[八島士奴美神]]との間に、[[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]]を産んでいる。
==その他== 新潟市の海辺の森には木花知流姫と名づけられた桜がある<ref>海辺の森 公式サイト 海辺の森日記[[2018年]][[4月12日]]</ref>。
==参考文献== {{Reflist}}
==関連項目== * [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] * [[八島士奴美神]] * [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] * [[木花之佐久夜毘売]]
{{デフォルトソート:このはなちるひめ}} [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]]

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月12日 (土) 08:20 (UTC)}} '''木花知流比売'''（このはなちるひめ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[女神]]。
==概要== [[古事記]]にのみ登場する[[国津神]]。
[[大山津見神]]は[[木花之佐久夜毘売]]や[[石長比売]]、[[神大市比売]]、[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|手名椎・足名椎]]などたくさんの神をもうけているが、木花知流比売もその一柱。八島士奴美神の妃となった。
名義は「桜の花が散ること」で、この性格は子の[[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]]にも受け継がれているとされる<ref>新潮社古典集成 古事記</ref>。
==系譜== [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]の娘の一人で、[[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]が大山津見神の孫の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛名田比売命]]を娶って産んだ神[[八島士奴美神]]との間に、[[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]]を産んでいる。
==その他== 新潟市の海辺の森には木花知流姫と名づけられた桜がある<ref>海辺の森 公式サイト 海辺の森日記[[2018年]][[4月12日]]</ref>。
==参考文献== {{Reflist}}
==関連項目== * [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] * [[八島士奴美神]] * [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] * [[木花之佐久夜毘売]]
{{デフォルトソート:このはなちるひめ}} [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]]

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月12日 (土) 08:20 (UTC)}} '''木花知流比売'''（このはなちるひめ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[女神]]。
==概要== 『[[古事記]]』に登場する[[国津神]]で、名称や系譜以外特に事績に関する記述はなく、『[[日本書紀]]』に記述はない。[[粟鹿神社]]の書物『[[粟鹿大明神元記]]』では'''木花知利比売'''（このはなちりひめ）と表記されている。
[[大山津見神]]は[[木花之佐久夜毘売]]や[[石長比売]]、[[神大市比売]]、[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|手名椎・足名椎]]などたくさんの神をもうけているが、木花知流比売もその一柱。[[八島士奴美神]]の妃となった。
「木花」は「桜の花」、「知流」は「散る」と解し、名義は「桜の花が散ること」で、この性格は子の[[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]]にも受け継がれていると考えられる<ref>新潮日本古典集成 古事記</ref>。
==系譜== [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]の娘の一人で、[[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]が大山津見神の孫の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛名田比売命]]を娶って産んだ神[[八島士奴美神]]との間に、[[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]]を産んでいる。
==その他== 新潟市の海辺の森には木花知流姫と名づけられた桜がある<ref>海辺の森 公式サイト 海辺の森日記[[2018年]][[4月12日]]</ref>。
==参考文献== {{Reflist}}
==関連項目== * [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] * [[八島士奴美神]] * [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] * [[木花之佐久夜毘売]]
{{デフォルトソート:このはなちるひめ}} [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]]

{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 木花知流比売 | 先代= | 次代= | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 木花知流比売 | 別名 = 木花知利比売 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの --> | 陵所 = | 父 = [[大山津見神]] | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = [[八島士奴美神]] | 子 = [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] | 宮 = | 神社 = }}
'''木花知流比売'''（このはなちるひめ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[女神]]。
==概要== 『[[古事記]]』に登場する[[国津神]]で、名称や系譜以外特に事績に関する記述はなく、『[[日本書紀]]』に記述はない。[[粟鹿神社]]の書物『[[粟鹿大明神元記]]』では'''木花知利比売'''（このはなちりひめ）と表記されている。
[[大山津見神]]は[[木花之佐久夜毘売]]や[[石長比売]]、[[神大市比売]]、[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|手名椎・足名椎]]などたくさんの神をもうけているが、木花知流比売もその一柱。[[八島士奴美神]]の妃となった。
「木花」は「桜の花」、「知流」は「散る」と解し、名義は「桜の花が散ること」で、この性格は子の[[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]]にも受け継がれていると考えられる<ref>新潮日本古典集成 古事記</ref>。
==系譜== [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]の娘の一人で、[[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]が大山津見神の孫の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛名田比売命]]を娶って産んだ神[[八島士奴美神]]との間に、[[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]]を産んでいる。
==その他== 新潟市の海辺の森には木花知流姫と名づけられた桜がある<ref>海辺の森 公式サイト 海辺の森日記[[2018年]][[4月12日]]</ref>。
==参考文献== {{Reflist}}
==関連項目== * [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] * [[八島士奴美神]] * [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] * [[木花之佐久夜毘売]]
{{デフォルトソート:このはなちるひめ}} [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]]

revision.text:
{{Expand English|date=2023年12月}} {{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 木花知流比売 | 先代 = | 次代 = | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 木花知流比売 | 別名 = 木花知利比売 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの --> | 陵所 = | 父 = [[大山津見神]] | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = [[八島士奴美神]] | 子 = [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] | 宮 = | 神社 = }} '''木花知流比売'''（このはなちるひめ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|女神]]。
== 概要 == 『[[古事記]]』に登場する[[国津神]]で、名称や系譜以外特に事績に関する記述はなく、『[[日本書紀]]』に記述はない。[[粟鹿神社]]の書物『[[粟鹿大明神元記]]』では'''木花知利比売'''（このはなちりひめ）と表記されている。
[[大山津見神]]は[[木花之佐久夜毘売]]・[[石長比売]]・[[神大市比売]]・[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|手名椎・足名椎]]などたくさんの神をもうけているが、木花知流比売もその一柱。[[八島士奴美神]]の妃となった。
「木花」は「桜の花」、「知流」は「散る」と解し、名義は「桜の花が散ること」で、この性格は子の[[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]]にも受け継がれていると考えられる<ref>新潮日本古典集成 古事記</ref>。
== 系譜 == [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]]の娘の一人で、[[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]が大山津見神の孫の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛名田比売命]]を娶って産んだ神[[八島士奴美神]]との間に、[[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]]を産んでいる。
== その他 == 新潟市の海辺の森には木花知流姫と名づけられた桜がある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url= http://umibenomori.com/blog/20180412113709.html |title=海辺の森日記 [[2018年]][[4月12日]] |publisher=海辺の森 公式サイト |accessdate=2021-2-15 }}</ref>。
== 参考文献 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] * [[八島士奴美神]] * [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] * [[木花之佐久夜毘売]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:このはなちるひめ}} [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:女神]] [[Category:十七世神]]

revision.text:
{{Expand English|date=2023年12月}} {{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 木花知流比売 | 先代 = | 次代 = | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 木花知流比売（コノハナチルヒメ） | 別名 = 木花知利比売 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->草木の神 | 陵所 = | 父 = [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = [[八島士奴美神]] | 子 = [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] | 宮 = | 神社 = | 記紀等 = 古事記 }} '''木花知流比売'''（木花知利比売）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|女神]]。 == 概要 == 『[[古事記]]』に登場する[[国津神]]で、名称や系譜以外特に事績に関する記述はなく、『[[日本書紀]]』に記述はない。[[粟鹿神社]]の書物『[[粟鹿大明神元記]]』では'''木花知利比売'''（コノハナチリヒメ）と表記されている。
大山津見神は[[コノハナノサクヤビメ|木花之佐久夜毘売]]・[[イワナガヒメ|石長比売]]・[[神大市比売]]・[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|手名椎・足名椎]]などの神をもうけており、木花知流比売もその一柱である。八島士奴美神の妃となった。
「木花」は「桜の花」、「知流」は「散る」と解し、名義は「桜の花が散ること」で、この性格は子の布波能母遅久奴須奴神にも受け継がれていると考えられる<ref>新潮日本古典集成 古事記</ref>。 == 系譜 == [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] 大山津見神の娘の一人で、[[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]が大山津見神の孫の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛名田比売命]]を娶って産んだ神八島士奴美神との間に、布波能母遅久奴須奴神を産んでいる。 == その他 == 新潟市の海辺の森には木花知流姫と名づけられた桜がある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url= http://umibenomori.com/blog/20180412113709.html |title=海辺の森日記 [[2018年]][[4月12日]] |publisher=海辺の森 公式サイト |accessdate=2021-2-15 }}</ref>。 == 参考文献 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[出雲族]] == 外部リンク == * [https://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/konohanachiruhime/ 木花知流比売 – 國學院大學 古典文化学事業] {{神道 横}} {{日本神話}} {{DEFAULTSORT:このはなちるひめ}} [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:女神]] [[Category:十七世神]] [[Category:草木の神]]

{{Expand English|date=2023年12月}} {{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 木花知流比売 | 先代 = | 次代 = | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 木花知流比売（コノハナチルヒメ） | 別名 = 木花知流比賣、木花知利比売、木花知利比賣 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->[[花]]の神 | 陵所 = | 父 = [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = [[八島士奴美神]] | 子 = [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] | 宮 = | 神社 = | 記紀等 = [[古事記]] }} '''木花知流比売'''（コノハナチルヒメ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|女神]]。 == 概要 == 『古事記』に登場する[[国津神]]で、名称や系譜以外特に事績に関する記述はなく、『[[日本書紀]]』に記述はない。[[粟鹿神社]]の書物『[[粟鹿大明神元記]]』では'''木花知利比売'''（コノハナチリヒメ）と表記されている。
大山津見神は[[コノハナノサクヤビメ|木花之佐久夜毘売]]・[[イワナガヒメ|石長比売]]・[[神大市比売]]・[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|手名椎・足名椎]]などの神をもうけており、木花知流比売もその一柱である。八島士奴美神の妃となった。
「木花」は「桜の花」、「知流」は「散る」と解し、名義は「桜の花が散ること」で、この性格は子の布波能母遅久奴須奴神にも受け継がれていると考えられる<ref>新潮日本古典集成 古事記</ref>。 == 系譜 == [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] 大山津見神の娘の一人で、[[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]が大山津見神の孫の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛名田比売命]]を娶って産んだ神八島士奴美神との間に、布波能母遅久奴須奴神を産んでいる。 == その他 == 新潟市の海辺の森には木花知流姫と名づけられた桜がある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url= http://umibenomori.com/blog/20180412113709.html |title=海辺の森日記 [[2018年]][[4月12日]] |publisher=海辺の森 公式サイト |accessdate=2021-02-15 }}</ref>。 == 参考文献 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[出雲族]] == 外部リンク == * [https://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/konohanachiruhime/ 木花知流比売 – 國學院大學 古典文化学事業] {{日本神話}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:このはなちるひめ}} [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:女神]] [[Category:十七世神]] [[Category:草木の神]]

{{Expand English|date=2023年12月}} {{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 木花知流比売 | 先代 = | 次代 = | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 木花知流比売（コノハナチルヒメ） | 別名 = 木花知流比賣、木花知利比売、木花知利比賣 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->[[花]]の神 | 陵所 = | 父 = [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = [[八島士奴美神]] | 子 = [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] | 宮 = | 神社 = | 記紀等 = [[古事記]] }} '''木花知流比売'''（コノハナチルヒメ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|女神]]。 == 概要 == 『古事記』に登場する[[国津神]]で、名称や系譜以外特に事績に関する記述はなく、『[[日本書紀]]』に記述はない。[[粟鹿神社]]の書物『[[粟鹿大明神元記]]』では'''木花知利比売'''（コノハナチリヒメ）と表記されている。
大山津見神は[[コノハナノサクヤビメ|木花之佐久夜毘売]]・[[イワナガヒメ|石長比売]]・[[神大市比売]]・[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|手名椎・足名椎]]などの神をもうけており、木花知流比売もその一柱である。八島士奴美神の妃となった。
「木花」は「桜の花」、「知流」は「散る」と解し、名義は「桜の花が散ること」で、この性格は子の布波能母遅久奴須奴神にも受け継がれていると考えられる{{Sfn|西宮|2014|p=372}}。 == 系譜 == [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] 大山津見神の娘の一人で、[[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]が大山津見神の孫の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛名田比売命]]を娶って産んだ神八島士奴美神との間に、布波能母遅久奴須奴神を産んでいる。 == その他 == [[新潟県]][[新潟市]][[北区 (新潟市)|北区]]の海辺の森には木花知流姫と名づけられた桜がある{{Sfn|海辺の森|2018}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * <!-- うみべのもり -->{{Cite web |url=https://www.umibenomori.com/blog/umibenomori.com/blog/20180412113709.html |title=「四季の森」の桜がもうすぐ開花します。 {{!}} 海辺の森 {{!}} 海辺の森 |access-date=2024-08-28 |publisher=特定非営利活動法人 森の会 |ref={{SfnRef|2018|海辺の森}} |date=2018-04-12 |author=海辺の森}} * <!-- にしみや -->{{Cite book|和書 |title=古事記 |volume= |date=2014年10月30日 |publisher=[[新潮社]] |ref={{SfnRef|西宮|2014}} |author=[[西宮一民]] 校注 |isbn=978-4-10-620801-0 |series=[[新潮日本古典集成]]（新装版）}} == 関連項目 == * [[十七世神]] == 外部リンク == * [https://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/konohanachiruhime/ 木花知流比売 – 國學院大學 古典文化学事業] {{日本神話}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:このはなちるひめ}} [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:女神]] [[Category:十七世神]] [[Category:草木の神]]

{{Expand English|date=2023年12月}} {{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 木花知流比売 | 先代 = | 次代 = | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 木花知流比売（コノハナチルヒメ） | 別名 = 木花知流比賣、木花知利比売、木花知利比賣 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->[[花]]の神 | 陵所 = | 父 = [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 兄弟姉妹 = {{Plainlist| * [[アメノサヅチ・クニノサヅチ|天之狭土神・国之狭土神]] * [[アメノサギリ・クニノサギリ|天之狭霧神・国之狭霧神]] * [[アメノクラト・クニノクラト|天之闇戸神・国之闇戸神]] * [[オオトマトイコ・オオトマトイメ|大戸惑子神・大戸惑女神]] * [[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|足名椎・手名椎]] * [[神大市比売]] * [[イワナガヒメ|石長火売]] * [[コノハナノサクヤビメ|木花之佐久夜毘売]] }} | 配偶者 = [[八島士奴美神]] | 子 = [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] | 宮 = | 神社 = | 記紀等 = [[古事記]] }} '''木花知流比売'''（コノハナチルヒメ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|女神]]。 == 概要 == 『古事記』に登場する[[国津神]]で、名称や系譜以外特に事績に関する記述はなく、『[[日本書紀]]』に記述はない。[[粟鹿神社]]の書物『[[粟鹿大明神元記]]』では'''木花知利比売'''（コノハナチリヒメ）と表記されている。

revision.text:
{{Expand English|date=2023年12月}} {{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 木花知流比売 | 先代 = | 次代 = | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 木花知流比売（コノハナチルヒメ） | 別名 = 木花知流比賣、木花知利比売、木花知利比賣 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->[[花]]の神 | 陵所 = | 父 = [[オオヤマツミ|大山津見神]] | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 兄弟姉妹 = {{Plainlist| * [[アメノサヅチ・クニノサヅチ|天之狭土神・国之狭土神]] * [[アメノサギリ・クニノサギリ|天之狭霧神・国之狭霧神]] * [[アメノクラト・クニノクラト|天之闇戸神・国之闇戸神]] * [[オオトマトイコ・オオトマトイメ|大戸惑子神・大戸惑女神]] * [[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|足名椎・手名椎]] * [[神大市比売]] * [[イワナガヒメ|石長比売]] * [[コノハナノサクヤビメ|木花之佐久夜毘売]] }} | 配偶者 = [[八島士奴美神]] | 子 = [[布波能母遅久奴須奴神]] | 宮 = | 神社 = | 記紀等 = [[古事記]] }} '''木花知流比売'''（コノハナチルヒメ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|女神]]。 == 概要 == 『古事記』に登場する[[国津神]]で、名称や系譜以外特に事績に関する記述はなく、『[[日本書紀]]』に記述はない。[[粟鹿神社]]の書物『[[粟鹿大明神元記]]』では'''木花知利比売'''（コノハナチリヒメ）と表記されている。
大山津見神は木花之佐久夜毘売・石長比売・神大市比売・手名椎・足名椎などの神をもうけており、木花知流比売もその一柱である。八島士奴美神の妃となった。
「木花」は「桜の花」、「知流」は「散る」と解し、名義は「桜の花が散ること」で、この性格は子の布波能母遅久奴須奴神にも受け継がれていると考えられる{{Sfn|西宮|2014|p=372}}。 == 系譜 == [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] 大山津見神の娘の一人で、[[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]が大山津見神の孫の[[クシナダヒメ|櫛名田比売命]]を娶って産んだ神八島士奴美神との間に、布波能母遅久奴須奴神を産んでいる。 == その他 == [[新潟県]][[新潟市]][[北区 (新潟市)|北区]]の海辺の森には木花知流姫と名づけられた桜がある{{Sfn|海辺の森|2018}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * <!-- うみべのもり -->{{Cite web |url=https://www.umibenomori.com/blog/umibenomori.com/blog/20180412113709.html |title=「四季の森」の桜がもうすぐ開花します。 {{!}} 海辺の森 {{!}} 海辺の森 |access-date=2024-08-28 |publisher=特定非営利活動法人 森の会 |ref={{SfnRef|2018|海辺の森}} |date=2018-04-12 |author=海辺の森}} * <!-- にしみや -->{{Cite book|和書 |title=古事記 |volume= |date=2014年10月30日 |publisher=[[新潮社]] |ref={{SfnRef|西宮|2014}} |author=[[西宮一民]] 校注 |isbn=978-4-10-620801-0 |series=[[新潮日本古典集成]]（新装版）}} == 関連項目 == * [[十七世神]] == 外部リンク == * [https://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/konohanachiruhime/ 木花知流比売 – 國學院大學 古典文化学事業] {{日本神話}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:このはなちるひめ}} [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:女神]] [[Category:十七世神]] [[Category:草木の神]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[木花知流比売]] to [[Konohanachiru-hime]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

| parents = {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|qid=Q386563}}

'''Konohanachiru-hime''' is a Japanese goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]]<ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Konohanachiru-hime • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/konohanachiruhime/ |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

Her name means "Blossoms-of- the-Trees-Falling-Princess".<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QEJUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Konohanachiru-hime&pg=PA510 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2021-12-06 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9179-7 |language=en}}</ref>

According to the [[kojiki]] she is the spouse of [[Yashimajinumi]] and daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="auto1">{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}} and thus and the mother of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}<ref name="Herbert-2010">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|277-278}} She is an ancestess of [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Konohanachiru-hime |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}

Her sister is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> the volcano goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] worshipped at [[Sengen shrine|Sengen shrines]]<ref>"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Go11ess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida.</ref> and wife of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref><ref>"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their we11ing. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."</ref>

While the [[Kojiki]] covers this narrative of the ancestry of Okuninushi.<ref>Philippi, Donald L. (2015). {{Lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}. Princeton University Press. p. 92.</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The [[:en:Yu-Gi-Oh!|Yu-Gi-Oh!]] card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/konohanachiruhime/ |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

Her name means "Blossoms-of- the-Trees-Falling-Princess".<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QEJUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Konohanachiru-hime&pg=PA510 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2021-12-06 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9179-7 |language=en}}</ref>

According to the [[kojiki]] she is the spouse of [[Yashimajinumi]] and daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="auto1">{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}} and thus and the mother of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}<ref name="Herbert-2010">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|277-278}} She is an ancestess of [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Konohanachiru-hime |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}

Her sister is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> the volcano goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] worshipped at [[Sengen shrine|Sengen shrines]]<ref>"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Go11ess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida.</ref> and wife of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref><ref>"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their we11ing. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."</ref>

While the [[Kojiki]] covers this narrative of the ancestry of Okuninushi.<ref>Philippi, Donald L. (2015). {{Lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}. Princeton University Press. p. 92.</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The [[:en:Yu-Gi-Oh!|Yu-Gi-Oh!]] card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The [[:en:Yu-Gi-Oh!|Yu-Gi-Oh!]] card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

'''Konohanachiru-hime''' is a Japanese goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]]<ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Konohanachiru-hime • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/konohanachiruhime/ |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

Her name means "Blossoms-of- the-Trees-Falling-Princess".<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QEJUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Konohanachiru-hime&pg=PA510 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2021-12-06 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9179-7 |language=en}}</ref>

According to the [[kojiki]] she is the spouse of [[Yashimajinumi]] and daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="auto1">{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}} and thus and the mother of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}<ref name="Herbert-2010">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|277-278}} She is an ancestess of [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Konohanachiru-hime |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}

Her sister is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> the volcano goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] worshipped at [[Sengen shrine|Sengen shrines]]<ref>"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Go11ess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida.</ref> and wife of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref><ref>"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their we11ing. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."</ref>

While the [[Kojiki]] covers this narrative of the ancestry of Okuninushi.<ref>Philippi, Donald L. (2015). {{Lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}. Princeton University Press. p. 92.</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The [[:en:Yu-Gi-Oh!|Yu-Gi-Oh!]] card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/konohanachiruhime/ |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

Her name means "Blossoms-of- the-Trees-Falling-Princess".<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QEJUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Konohanachiru-hime&pg=PA510 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2021-12-06 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9179-7 |language=en}}</ref>

According to the [[kojiki]] she is the spouse of [[Yashimajinumi]] and daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="auto1">{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}} and thus and the mother of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}<ref name="Herbert-2010">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|277-278}} She is an ancestess of [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Konohanachiru-hime |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}

Her sister is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> the volcano goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] worshipped at [[Sengen shrine|Sengen shrines]]<ref>"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Go11ess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida.</ref> and wife of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref><ref>"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their we11ing. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."</ref>

While the [[Kojiki]] covers this narrative of the ancestry of Okuninushi.<ref>Philippi, Donald L. (2015). {{Lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}. Princeton University Press. p. 92.</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The [[:en:Yu-Gi-Oh!|Yu-Gi-Oh!]] card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

[http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The [[:en:Yu-Gi-Oh!|Yu-Gi-Oh!]] card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

'''Konohanachiru-hime''' is a Japanese goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]]<ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Konohanachiru-hime • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/konohanachiruhime/ |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

Her name means "Blossoms-of- the-Trees-Falling-Princess".<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QEJUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Konohanachiru-hime&pg=PA510 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2021-12-06 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9179-7 |language=en}}</ref>

According to the [[kojiki]] she is the spouse of [[Yashimajinumi]] and daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="auto1">{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}} and thus and the mother of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}<ref name="Herbert-2010">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|277-278}} She is an ancestess of [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Konohanachiru-hime |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}

Her sister is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> the volcano goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] worshipped at [[Sengen shrine|Sengen shrines]]<ref>"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Go11ess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida.</ref> and wife of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref><ref>"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their we11ing. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."</ref>

While the [[Kojiki]] covers this narrative of the ancestry of Okuninushi.<ref>Philippi, Donald L. (2015). {{Lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}. Princeton University Press. p. 92.</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The [[:en:Yu-Gi-Oh!|Yu-Gi-Oh!]] card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

[http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/konohanachiruhime/ |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

Her name means "Blossoms-of- the-Trees-Falling-Princess".<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QEJUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Konohanachiru-hime&pg=PA510 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2021-12-06 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9179-7 |language=en}}</ref>

According to the [[kojiki]] she is the spouse of [[Yashimajinumi]] and daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="auto1">{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}} and thus and the mother of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}<ref name="Herbert-2010">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|277-278}} She is an ancestess of [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Konohanachiru-hime |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}

Her sister is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> the volcano goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] worshipped at [[Sengen shrine|Sengen shrines]]<ref>"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Go11ess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]] of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida.</ref> and wife of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref><ref>"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their we11ing. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."</ref>

While the [[Kojiki]] covers this narrative of the ancestry of Okuninushi.<ref>Philippi, Donald L. (2015). {{Lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}. Princeton University Press. p. 92.</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The [[:en:Yu-Gi-Oh!|Yu-Gi-Oh!]] card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

[http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The [[:en:Yu-Gi-Oh!|Yu-Gi-Oh!]] card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

'''Konohanachiru-hime''' is a Japanese goddess<ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Konohanachiru-hime • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/konohanachiruhime/ |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

Her name means "Blossoms-of- the-Trees-Falling-Princess".<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QEJUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Konohanachiru-hime&pg=PA510 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2021-12-06 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9179-7 |language=en}}</ref>

According to the [[kojiki]] she is the spouse of [[Yashimajinumi]] and daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="auto1">{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}} and thus and the mother of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}<ref name="Herbert-2010">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|277-278}} She is an ancestess of [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Konohanachiru-hime |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}

Her sister is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> the volcano goddess worshipped at [[Sengen shrine|Sengen shrines]]<ref>"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Go11ess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida.</ref> and wife of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref><ref>"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their we11ing. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."</ref>

While the [[Kojiki]] covers this narrative of the ancestry of Okuninushi.<ref>Philippi, Donald L. (2015). {{Lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}. Princeton University Press. p. 92.</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The [[:en:Yu-Gi-Oh!|Yu-Gi-Oh!]] card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/konohanachiruhime/ |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

Her name means "Blossoms-of- the-Trees-Falling-Princess".<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QEJUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Konohanachiru-hime&pg=PA510 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2021-12-06 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9179-7 |language=en}}</ref>

According to the [[kojiki]] she is the spouse of [[Yashimajinumi]] and daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="auto1">{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}} and thus and the mother of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}<ref name="Herbert-2010">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|277-278}} She is an ancestess of [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Konohanachiru-hime |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}

Her sister is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> the volcano goddess worshipped at [[Sengen shrine|Sengen shrines]]<ref>"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Go11ess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida.</ref> and wife of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref><ref>"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their we11ing. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."</ref>

While the [[Kojiki]] covers this narrative of the ancestry of Okuninushi.<ref>Philippi, Donald L. (2015). {{Lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}. Princeton University Press. p. 92.</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The [[:en:Yu-Gi-Oh!|Yu-Gi-Oh!]] card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref><ref>"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their we11ing. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."</ref>

While the [[Kojiki]] covers this narrative of the ancestry of Okuninushi.<ref>Philippi, Donald L. (2015). {{Lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}. Princeton University Press. p. 92.</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The {{ill|Yu-Gi-Oh!|en|Yu-Gi-Oh!|WD=Q192015|lt=Yu-Gi-Oh!}} card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>
