{{Short description|Japanese goddess}}

{{Cleanup|reason=general prose cleanup would be helpful here|date=November 2023}}
{{infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| children = {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}<ref name="auto1" /><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}
| father = [[Ōyamatsumi|Oyamatsumi]]<ref name="auto1" /><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}
| consort =[[Yashimajinumi]]<ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}; {{ill|Yashimajinumi no kami|WD=Q55522907}}| mother = 
| gender = {{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}
| parents = {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563}}
| offspring = {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|WD=Q65266248}}
}}

'''Konohanachiru-hime''' is a Japanese goddess<ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Konohanachiru-hime • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/konohanachiruhime/ |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

Her name means "Blossoms-of- the-Trees-Falling-Princess".<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QEJUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Konohanachiru-hime&pg=PA510 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2021-12-06 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9179-7 |language=en}}</ref>

According to the [[kojiki]] she is the spouse of [[Yashimajinumi]] and daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="auto1">{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}} and thus and the mother of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}<ref name="Herbert-2010">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|277-278}} She is an ancestess of [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Konohanachiru-hime |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}

Her sister is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> the volcano goddess worshipped at [[Sengen shrine|Sengen shrines]]<ref>"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Go11ess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida.</ref> and wife of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref><ref>"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their we11ing. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign {{ill|Bedroom|en|Bedroom|WD=Q193837|lt=bower}} and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."</ref>

While the [[Kojiki]] covers this narrative of the ancestry of Okuninushi.<ref>Philippi, Donald L. (2015). {{Lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}. Princeton University Press. p. 92.</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The {{ill|Yu-Gi-Oh!|en|Yu-Gi-Oh!|WD=Q192015|lt=Yu-Gi-Oh!}} card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/konohanachiruhime/ |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

Her name means "Blossoms-of- the-Trees-Falling-Princess".<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QEJUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Konohanachiru-hime&pg=PA510 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2021-12-06 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9179-7 |language=en}}</ref>

According to the [[kojiki]] she is the spouse of [[Yashimajinumi]] and daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="auto1">{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}} and thus and the mother of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}<ref name="Herbert-2010">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|277-278}} She is an ancestess of [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Konohanachiru-hime |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}

Her sister is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> the volcano goddess worshipped at [[Sengen shrine|Sengen shrines]]<ref>"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Go11ess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida.</ref> and wife of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref><ref>"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their we11ing. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign {{ill|Bedroom|en|Bedroom|WD=Q193837|lt=bower}} and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."</ref>

While the [[Kojiki]] covers this narrative of the ancestry of Okuninushi.<ref>Philippi, Donald L. (2015). {{Lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}. Princeton University Press. p. 92.</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The {{ill|Yu-Gi-Oh!|en|Yu-Gi-Oh!|WD=Q192015|lt=Yu-Gi-Oh!}} card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

(Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The {{ill|Yu-Gi-Oh!|en|Yu-Gi-Oh!|WD=Q192015|lt=Yu-Gi-Oh!|1=Yu-Gi-Oh!}} card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

{{Short description|Japanese goddess}}

{{infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| children = {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}<ref name="auto1" /><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}

| consort =[[Yashimajinumi]]<ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}; {{ill|Yashimajinumi no kami|WD=Q55522907|1=Yashimajinumi}}| mother =

| parents = {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563|1=Ōyamatsumi}}

'''Konohanachiru-hime''' is a Japanese goddess<ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Konohanachiru-hime • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/konohanachiruhime/ |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

Her name means "Blossoms-of- the-Trees-Falling-Princess".<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QEJUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Konohanachiru-hime&pg=PA510 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2021-12-06 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9179-7 |language=en}}</ref>

According to the [[kojiki]] she is the spouse of [[Yashimajinumi]] and daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="auto1">{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}} and thus and the mother of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}<ref name="Herbert-2010">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|277-278}} She is an ancestess of [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Konohanachiru-hime |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}

Her sister is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> the volcano goddess worshipped at [[Sengen shrine|Sengen shrines]]<ref>"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Go11ess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida.</ref> and wife of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref><ref>"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their we11ing. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign {{ill|Bedroom|en|Bedroom|WD=Q193837|lt=bower|1=Bedroom}} and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."</ref>

While the [[Kojiki]] covers this narrative of the ancestry of Okuninushi.<ref>Philippi, Donald L. (2015). {{Lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}. Princeton University Press. p. 92.</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The {{ill|Yu-Gi-Oh!|en|Yu-Gi-Oh!|WD=Q192015|lt=Yu-Gi-Oh!|1=Yu-Gi-Oh!}} card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/konohanachiruhime/ |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

Her name means "Blossoms-of- the-Trees-Falling-Princess".<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QEJUEAAAQBAJ&dq=Konohanachiru-hime&pg=PA510 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2021-12-06 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9179-7 |language=en}}</ref>

According to the [[kojiki]] she is the spouse of [[Yashimajinumi]] and daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="auto1">{{Cite web |date=2007-09-30 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930182726/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}} and thus and the mother of {{ill|Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|ja|布波能母遅久奴須奴神|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|1=Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuha-no-Mojikunusunu|qq=}}<ref name="Herbert-2010">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|277-278}} She is an ancestess of [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Konohanachiru-hime |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Herbert-2010" />{{rp|277-278}}

Her sister is [[Konohanasakuya-hime]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> the volcano goddess worshipped at [[Sengen shrine|Sengen shrines]]<ref>"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Go11ess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida.</ref> and wife of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref><ref>"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their we11ing. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign {{ill|Bedroom|en|Bedroom|qid=Q193837|lt=bower|1=Bedroom}} and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."</ref>

While the [[Kojiki]] covers this narrative of the ancestry of Okuninushi.<ref>Philippi, Donald L. (2015). {{Lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}. Princeton University Press. p. 92.</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013">{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|29–30}} The [[Nihon Shoki]] on the other hand omits the entire narrative of the ancestry of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] from [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and has him as a direct son, skipping the section where Konohanachiru-hime is mentioned,<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=54|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref name="Maxey-2013" />{{rp|29–30}} however it references the kojiki alternative narrative.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=56|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

The {{ill|Yu-Gi-Oh!|en|Yu-Gi-Oh!|qid=Q192015|lt=Yu-Gi-Oh!|1=Yu-Gi-Oh!}} card Chirubimé is based on her.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Chirubimé, Princess of Autumn Leaves - Baza Kart Yu-Gi-Oh! - YuGiOh.pl |url=https://yugioh.pl/karta/Chirubim%C3%A9,_Princess_of_Autumn_Leaves |access-date=2023-11-14 |website=yugioh.pl}}</ref>
